bibdata.title: OGC Emission Event Modeling Language (EmissionML) Part I: Data Model

localized-strings.localized-string: Terms, definitions, symbols and abbreviated terms
No terms and definitions are listed in this document.

This document uses the terms defined in https://portal.ogc.org/public_ogc/directives/directives.php[OGC Policy Directive 49], which is based on the ISO/IEC Directives, Part 2, Rules for the structure and drafting of International Standards. In particular, the word “shall” (not “must”) is the verb form used to indicate a requirement to be strictly followed to conform to this document and OGC documents do not use the equivalent phrases in the ISO/IEC Directives, Part 2.
This document also uses terms defined in the OGC Standard for Modular specifications (https://portal.opengeospatial.org/files/?artifact_id=34762[OGC 08-131r3]), also known as the 'ModSpec'. The definitions of terms such as standard, specification, requirement, and conformance test are provided in the ModSpec.
For the purposes of this document, the following additional terms and definitions apply.

The following documents are referred to in the text in such a way that some or all of their content constitutes requirements of this document. For dated references, only the edition cited applies. For undated references, the latest edition of the referenced document (including any amendments) applies.
There are no normative references in this document.

This document uses the terms defined in https://portal.ogc.org/public_ogc/directives/directives.php[OGC Policy Directive 49], which is based on the ISO/IEC Directives, Part 2, Rules for the structure and drafting of International Standards. In particular, the word “shall” (not “must”) is the verb form used to indicate a requirement to be strictly followed to conform to this document and OGC documents do not use the equivalent phrases in the ISO/IEC Directives, Part 2.
This document also uses terms defined in the OGC Standard for Modular specifications (https://portal.opengeospatial.org/files/?artifact_id=34762[OGC 08-131r3]), also known as the 'ModSpec'. The definitions of terms such as standard, specification, requirement, and conformance test are provided in the ModSpec.
For the purposes of this document, the terms and definitions given in % additionally apply.

This document uses the terms defined in https://portal.ogc.org/public_ogc/directives/directives.php[OGC Policy Directive 49], which is based on the ISO/IEC Directives, Part 2, Rules for the structure and drafting of International Standards. In particular, the word “shall” (not “must”) is the verb form used to indicate a requirement to be strictly followed to conform to this document and OGC documents do not use the equivalent phrases in the ISO/IEC Directives, Part 2.
This document also uses terms defined in the OGC Standard for Modular specifications (https://portal.opengeospatial.org/files/?artifact_id=34762[OGC 08-131r3]), also known as the 'ModSpec'. The definitions of terms such as standard, specification, requirement, and conformance test are provided in the ModSpec.
For the purposes of this document, the terms and definitions given in % and the following additionally apply.
No security considerations have been made for this document.

clause.title: Copyright notice
clause.p:
Copyright © 2026 Open Geospatial Consortium
To obtain additional rights of use, visit

clause.title: Note
clause.p: Attention is drawn to the possibility that some of the elements of this document may be the subject of patent rights. The Open Geospatial Consortium shall not be held responsible for identifying any or all such patent rights.

Recipients of this document are requested to submit, with their comments, notification of any relevant patent claims or other intellectual property rights of which they may be aware that might be infringed by any implementation of the standard set forth in this document, and to provide supporting documentation.

clause.title: License Agreement
clause.p: Use of this document is subject to the license agreement at

clause.title: Notice
clause.p: This document is not an OGC Standard. This document is an OGC Draft Standard and is therefore not an official position of the OGC membership. It is distributed for review and comment. It is subject to change without notice and may not be referred to as an OGC Standard.
Further, an OGC Draft Standard should not be referenced as required or mandatory technology in procurements.

clause.title: Keywords
clause.p: The following are keywords to be used by search engines and document catalogues.
ogcdoc, OGC document, emissions, climate change

foreword.title: Preface
foreword.p: The following are keywords to be used by search engines and document catalogues.
ogcdoc, OGC document, <tags separated by commas>

note.p: Insert Preface Text here. Give OGC specific commentary: describe the technical content, reason for document, history of the document and precursors, and plans for future work. > Attention is drawn to the possibility that some of the elements of this document may be the subject of patent rights. The Open Geospatial Consortium shall not be held responsible for identifying any or all such patent rights.

Recipients of this document are requested to submit, with their comments, notification of any relevant patent claims or other intellectual property rights of which they may be aware that might be infringed by any implementation of the standard set forth in this document, and to provide supporting documentation.

foreword.p: No security considerations have been made for this Standard.
The following organizations submitted this Document to the Open Geospatial Consortium (OGC):
All questions regarding this submission should be directed to the editor or the submitters:

clause.title: Security considerations
clause.p: No security considerations have been made for this document.

clause.title: Submitting Organizations
clause.p: The following organizations submitted this Document to the Open Geospatial Consortium (OGC):

note.p: Insert Scope text here. Give the subject of the document and the aspects of that scope covered by the document.

terms.title: Terms and definitions
terms.p: No terms and definitions are listed in this document.

This document uses the terms defined in OGC Policy Directive 49OGC Policy Directive 49, which is based on the ISO/IEC Directives, Part 2, Rules for the structure and drafting of International Standards. In particular, the word “shall” (not “must”) is the verb form used to indicate a requirement to be strictly followed to conform to this Standard and OGC documents do not use the equivalent phrases in the ISO/IEC Directives, Part 2.

This document also uses terms defined in the OGC Standard for Modular specifications (OGC 08-131r3OGC 08-131r3), also known as the ‘ModSpec’. The definitions of terms such as standard, specification, requirement, and conformance test are provided in the ModSpec.
For the purposes of this document, the following additional terms and definitions apply.

dd.p: A Determination Procedure describes the steps performed to determine the quantity or temporal bounds of an Emission Event.

An Emission Event represents the release of a substance into the atmosphere over a non-zero duration. It is characterized by its start time and end time, the Source Feature from which the substance originated, and the identity and quantity of the emitted substance.
An Emission Quantity is the physical quantity representing the total mass of substance released to the atmosphere during a defined Emission Event or over a specified time interval.
Emission Intent is a code list describing whether an emission is intentional, unintentional, or unknown.
Abstraction of real world phenomena (Source: ISO 19101-1:2014)
Set of operations having the object of determining the value of a quantity (Source: ISO/OGC 19156:2023ISO/OGC 19156:2023)

A Mechanism is a classification of the specific physical, chemical, or operational process that constitutes the direct cause or pathway for an emission. It is defined independently of the Source—including its identity, structure, or purpose—allowing Mechanisms to be consistently reused across different types of Source.

Act carried out by an observer to determine the value of an observable property of an object (feature-of-interest) by using a procedure, with the value provided as the result (Source: ISO/OGC 19156:2023ISO/OGC 19156:2023)

A Source represents the feature from which an emission originates. A Source is an ISO 191xx feature whose geometry provides the geospatial reference for one or more Emission Events. A Source is characterised by its own identity, geometry, intended function, and lifecycle, and is explicitly agnostic to the Mechanism by which any associated Emission Event occurs.
A Source Type classifies a Source by its category.
A Temporal Bound is a value object representing a temporal determination (e.g., start time or end time) that MAY have associated data quality metadata.
Reference quantity chosen from a unit equivalence group (Source: ISO/OGC 19156:2023ISO/OGC 19156:2023)

clause.title: Clauses not Containing Normative Material
clause.p: Paragraph

clause.title: Clauses not containing normative material sub-clause 1

fmt-title.semx: Clauses not containing normative material sub-clause 1

clause.p: Paragraph

clause.title: Clauses not containing normative material sub-clause 2

fmt-title.semx: Clauses not containing normative material sub-clause 2

clause.title: Emission Event Data Model
clause.p: The EmissionML conceptual model is described as a class diagram in Figure 1. The schema is fully described in x.x.x.

fmt-name.semx: EmissionML Abstract Conceptual Model

table.name: UML relationship notation used in this standard

fmt-name.semx: UML relationship notation used in this standard

tr.td: The target is owned by and cannot exist without the source (e.g., EmissionEvent owns its EmissionQuantity and TemporalBound instances)
A directional reference between independent classes (e.g., EmissionEvent refers to a Source)

clause.p: Use the following table for Requirements Classes.

td.strong: Emission Event Conceptual Data Model Core

tr.td: This requirement defines the Emission Event.
This requirement defines the Source Type.
This requirement defines the Emission Quantity.
This requirement defines the Unit of Measure.
This requirement defines the Temporal Bound.
This requirement defines the Emission Intent.
This requirement defines the Determination Procedure.

clause.title: Emission Event Class
clause.p: An Emission Event represents the release of a substance into the atmosphere over a non-zero duration. It is characterized by its start time and end time, the Source Feature from which the substance originated, and the identity and quantity of the emitted substance.

Note: Conceptually, an Emission Event corresponds to an occurrent in upper-ontology terms, as it unfolds over time and has temporal parts. Note: For reporting or accounting purposes, a long-running emission event is often segmented into multiple time-bounded emission events (e.g., annual records). This segmentation reflects reporting conventions and does not imply physical cessation or re-initiation of the emission.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/emission-event
Each Emission Event class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in Table 5. Each Emission Event class shall have the direct relation between an Emission Event class and other classes listed in Table 6.

table.name: Attributes of an Emission Event class

fmt-name.semx: Attributes of an Emission Event class

tr.td: The URI linking to an external definition of the pollutantType of the Emission Event.

table.name: Direct relation between an Emission Event class and other class types

fmt-name.semx: Direct relation between an Emission Event class and other class types

tr.td: The mechanism by which the emission occurred.
The source from which the emission originated.
The start time of the emission event.
The end time of the emission event.
The quantity of the substance emitted.
The procedure used to determine the start time.
The procedure used to determine the end time.
The procedure used to determine the emission quantity.
Observations providing evidence for the start time determination.
Observations providing evidence for the end time determination.
Observations providing evidence for the quantity determination.

clause.title: Feature Class
clause.p: A Feature is an abstraction of real world phenomena (Source: ISO 19101-1:2014). In EmissionML, Feature serves as the base class for entities that can be the ultimate feature of interest of an Observation. A Feature’s geometry attribute provides the geospatial reference for the phenomenon it represents.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/feature
+ Each Feature class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in Table 8.

table.name: Attributes of a Feature class

fmt-name.semx: Attributes of a Feature class

tr.td: The spatial geometry of the feature.

table.name: Direct relation between a Feature class and other class types

fmt-name.semx: Direct relation between a Feature class and other class types

tr.td: No direct relations are defined for Feature in the core model (it is referenced by Source via inheritance and by Observation via ultimateFeatureOfInterest).

clause.title: Source Class
clause.p: A Source represents the feature from which an emission originates. A Source is an ISO 191xx feature whose geometry provides the geospatial reference for one or more Emission Events. A Source is characterised by its own identity, geometry, intended function, and lifecycle, and is explicitly agnostic to the Mechanism by which any associated Emission Event occurs.

Note: A Source is a continuant that may exist independently of any emission, whereas an Emission Event is an occurrent. The identity and lifecycle of a Source are independent of the existence, absence, or frequency of Emission Events.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/source
+ Each Source class shall inherit from Feature. Each Source class shall have the optional attributes listed in Table 11. Each Source class shall have the direct relation between a Source class and other classes listed in Table 12.

table.name: Attributes of a Source class (own attributes; geometry is inherited from Feature)

fmt-name.semx: Attributes of a Source class (own attributes; geometry is inherited from Feature)

tr.td: A human-readable name for the source.

table.name: Direct relation between a Source class and other class types

fmt-name.semx: Direct relation between a Source class and other class types

tr.td: The type classification of the source.

clause.title: Source Type Class
clause.p: A Source Type classifies a Source by its category.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/source-type
+ Each Source Type class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in Table 14.

table.name: Attributes of a Source Type class

fmt-name.semx: Attributes of a Source Type class

tr.td: A human-readable label for the source type.
A detailed description of the source type.
The URI linking to an external definition of the source type.

table.name: Direct relation between a Source Type class and other class types

fmt-name.semx: Direct relation between a Source Type class and other class types

tr.td: No direct relations are defined for SourceType in the core model (it is referenced by Source).

clause.title: Mechanism Class
clause.p: A Mechanism is a classification of the specific physical, chemical, or operational process that constitutes the direct cause or pathway for an emission. It is defined independently of the Source—including its identity, structure, or purpose—allowing Mechanisms to be consistently reused across different types of Source.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/mechanism
+ Each Mechanism class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in Table 17. Each Mechanism class shall have the direct relation listed in Table 18.

table.name: Attributes of a Mechanism class

fmt-name.semx: Attributes of a Mechanism class

tr.td: A human-readable label for the mechanism.
A detailed description of the mechanism.

table.name: Direct relation between a Mechanism class and other class types

fmt-name.semx: Direct relation between a Mechanism class and other class types

tr.td: The emission intent associated with the mechanism.

clause.title: Emission Quantity Class
clause.p: An Emission Quantity is the physical quantity representing the total mass of substance released to the atmosphere during a defined Emission Event or over a specified time interval.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/emission-quantity
+ Each Emission Quantity class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in Table 20. Each Emission Quantity class shall have the direct relation between an Emission Quantity class and other classes listed in Table 21.

table.name: Attributes of an Emission Quantity class

fmt-name.semx: Attributes of an Emission Quantity class

tr.td: The numeric value of the emission quantity.
A data quality element describing the quality of the emission quantity value.

table.name: Direct relation between an Emission Quantity class and other class types

fmt-name.semx: Direct relation between an Emission Quantity class and other class types

tr.td: The unit of measure associated with the emission quantity.

clause.title: Unit of Measure Class
clause.p: A Unit of Measure is a reference quantity chosen from a unit equivalence group (Source: ISO/OGC 19156:2023)

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/unit-of-measure
+ Each Unit of Measure class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in Table 23.

table.name: Attributes of a Unit of Measure class

fmt-name.semx: Attributes of a Unit of Measure class

tr.td: A human-readable label for the unit of measure.
A short symbol representing the unit of measure.
The URI linking to an external definition of the unit of measure.

table.name: Direct relation between a Unit of Measure class and other class types

fmt-name.semx: Direct relation between a Unit of Measure class and other class types

tr.td: No direct relations are defined for UnitOfMeasure in the core model.

clause.title: Temporal Bound Class
clause.p: A Temporal Bound is a value object representing a temporal determination (e.g., start time or end time) that MAY have associated data quality metadata. The determination procedure and supporting evidence for a Temporal Bound are tracked at the Emission Event level.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/temporal-bound
+ Each Temporal Bound class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in Table 26.

table.name: Attributes of a Temporal Bound class

fmt-name.semx: Attributes of a Temporal Bound class

tr.td: The time instant value of this Temporal Bound.
A data quality element describing the quality of the temporal bound.

table.name: Direct relation between a Temporal Bound class and other class types

fmt-name.semx: Direct relation between a Temporal Bound class and other class types

tr.td: No direct relations are defined for TemporalBound in the core model (determination procedure and evidence are tracked at the EmissionEvent level).

clause.title: Observation Class
clause.p: An Observation is an act carried out by an observer to determine the value of an observable property of an object (feature-of-interest) by using a procedure, with the value provided as the result (Source: ISO/OGC 19156:2023). In EmissionML, an Observation is evidence that may support an Emission Quantity or Temporal Bound determination.

Note: The Ultimate Feature of Interest of an Observation identifies a Feature — which may be a Source or another feature at a different spatial scale — associated with the Emission Event. It is common for a single Emission Event to be observed by multiple Observations whose Ultimate Features of Interest are defined at different spatial scales. For example, a satellite remote sensing image Observation typically has an area-based Ultimate Feature of Interest (e.g., a facility area or region), whereas the same Emission Event may also be observed by a person carrying an Optical Gas Imaging camera, for which the Ultimate Feature of Interest is a specific Source such as a component of an item of equipment from which pollutants are released.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/observation
+ Each Observation class shall be an ISO/OGC 19156:2023 (OGC OMS 3.0) Observation.

clause.title: Emission Intent Enumeration
clause.p: Emission Intent is a code list describing whether an emission is intentional, unintentional, or unknown.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/emission-intent
+ The Emission Intent enumeration shall support the enumeration values listed in Table 30.

table.name: Enumeration values of Emission Intent

fmt-name.semx: Enumeration values of Emission Intent

tr.td: The intent of the emission is unknown.

clause.title: Procedure Class
clause.p: A Procedure is a description of steps performed (Source: ISO/OGC 19156:2023). In EmissionML, Procedure serves as the base class for process descriptions such as DeterminationProcedure.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/procedure
+ Each Procedure class shall be an ISO/OGC 19156:2023 (OGC OMS 3.0) Procedure.

table.name: Attributes of a Procedure class

fmt-name.semx: Attributes of a Procedure class

tr.td: No own attributes are defined for Procedure in the core model (see the standard for complete specification).

table.name: Direct relation between a Procedure class and other class types

fmt-name.semx: Direct relation between a Procedure class and other class types

tr.td: No direct relations are defined for Procedure in the core model (it is referenced by DeterminationProcedure via inheritance).

clause.title: Determination Procedure Class
clause.p: A Determination Procedure describes the steps performed to determine the quantity or temporal bounds of an Emission Event. DeterminationProcedure inherits from Procedure, aligning it with the OMS 3.0 process model.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/determination-procedure
+ Each Determination Procedure class shall inherit from Procedure. Each Determination Procedure class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in Table 35.

table.name: Attributes of a Determination Procedure class (own attributes)

fmt-name.semx: Attributes of a Determination Procedure class (own attributes)

tr.td: A human-readable label for the determination procedure.
A detailed description of the determination procedure.
The URI linking to an external definition of the determination procedure.

references.title: Normative references
references.p: There are no normative references in this document.

The following normative documents contain provisions that, through reference in this text, constitute provisions of this document. For dated references, subsequent amendments to, or revisions of, any of these publications do not apply. For undated references, the latest edition of the normative document referred to applies.NOTE:NoteClause 2, Note
Insert References here. If there are no references, state “There are no normative references”.
References are to follow the Springer LNCS style, with the exception that optional information may be appended to references: DOIs are added after the date and web resource references may include an access date at the end of the reference in parentheses. See examples from Springer and OGC below.
Smith, T.F., Waterman, M.S.: Identification of Common Molecular Subsequences. J. Mol. Biol. 147, 195–197 (1981)
May, P., Ehrlich, H.C., Steinke, T.: ZIB Structure Prediction Pipeline: Composing a Complex Biological Workflow through Web Services. In: Nagel, W.E., Walter, W.V., Lehner, W. (eds.) Euro-Par 2006. LNCS, vol. 4128, pp. 1148–1158. Springer, Heidelberg (2006)
Foster, I., Kesselman, C.: The Grid: Blueprint for a New Computing Infrastructure. Morgan Kaufmann, San Francisco (1999)
Czajkowski, K., Fitzgerald, S., Foster, I., Kesselman, C.: Grid Information Services for Distributed Resource Sharing. In: 10th IEEE International Symposium on High Performance Distributed Computing, pp. 181–184. IEEE Press, New York (2001)
Foster, I., Kesselman, C., Nick, J., Tuecke, S.: The Physiology of the Grid: an Open Grid Services Architecture for Distributed Systems Integration. Technical report, Global Grid Forum (2002)
National Center for Biotechnology Information,
ISO / TC 211: ISO 19115-1:2014 Geographic information — Metadata — Part 1: Fundamentals (2014)
ISO / TC 211: ISO 19157:2013 Geographic information — Data quality (2013)
ISO / TC 211: ISO 19139:2007 Geographic information — Metadata — XML schema implementation (2007)
ISO / TC 211: ISO 19115-3: Geographic information — Metadata — Part 3: XML schemas (2016)
OGC: OGC 15-097 OGC Geospatial User Feedback Standard. Conceptual Model (2016)
OGC: OGC 12-019, OGC City Geography Markup Language (CityGML) Encoding Standard (2012)
OGC: OGC 14-005r3, OGC IndoorGML (2014)

title.strong: Relationship with OGC Features and Observations

semx.strong: Relationship with OGC Features and Observations

annex.p: EmissionML is a domain-specific conceptual model that builds on two foundational OGC concepts: Features (ISO 19109 General Feature Model) and Observations (ISO/OGC 19156:2023, OMS 3.0). This annex explains how EmissionML relates to each and why explicit domain modeling is preferred over generic usage.
Figure A.1 illustrates the conceptual layering of EmissionML on top of these foundational standards.

figure.name: Conceptual layering of EmissionML on foundational ISO/OGC standards

fmt-name.semx: Conceptual layering of EmissionML on foundational ISO/OGC standards

clause.title: EmissionML and OGC Features
clause.p: The OGC General Feature Model (ISO 19109) defines a Feature as an abstraction of a real-world phenomenon — any entity with an identity, properties, and optionally a geometry. EmissionML makes this alignment explicit by introducing a concrete Feature class (stereotyped [ISO_19109]) into the conceptual model.

The Feature class carries a mandatory geometry attribute of type GM_Object and serves as the base class for domain-specific feature types. Source inherits from Feature, gaining its geometry by inheritance. This structural inheritance — rather than a notional stereotype — makes the ISO 19109 alignment unambiguous and machine-verifiable.

Because Feature is concrete, an Observation’s ultimateFeatureOfInterest can reference any Feature — not only a Source. This enables observations whose feature of interest is defined at a different spatial scale (e.g., a facility area, a region, or a monitoring zone) to participate in the evidence chain without being forced into the Source class.

EmissionEvent is also a real-world phenomenon — an occurrent that unfolds over time. In principle, an Emission Event could be modeled as a generic Feature. However, EmissionML gives it explicit structure: temporal bounds (start time, end time), a quantity of emitted substance, a mechanism describing the physical or operational process, an intent classification, and an evidence chain linking the event to supporting Observations.

Without this explicit modeling, a Source would be indistinguishable from any other Feature, and an emission event would be “just another Feature” with ad-hoc properties. The semantic relationships that give emission data its meaning — hasSource, hasMechanism, hasQuantity, hasStartTimeEvidence — would be lost or require convention-based interpretation.

clause.title: EmissionML and OMS 3.0 Observations
clause.p: OMS 3.0 (ISO/OGC 19156:2023) defines an Observation as an act of determining the value of an observable property. An Observation has a well-defined structure: a feature of interest, an observed property, a procedure, and a result.

EmissionML incorporates OMS 3.0 Observation by reference (Requirement 8) — it does not redefine or extend the Observation class. In EmissionML, Observations serve as evidence supporting emission quantity and temporal bound determinations, linked through the hasQuantityEvidence, hasStartTimeEvidence, and hasEndTimeEvidence associations.

One might ask: Why not model the entire emission event as an Observation result? Because an Emission Event is richer than what a single Observation captures. An Emission Event has a source, a mechanism, an intent, multiple temporal bounds, and can be supported by multiple Observations at different spatial and temporal scales. For example:

li.p: A satellite remote sensing Observation may detect a methane plume over a broad area.
An Optical Gas Imaging (OGI) camera Observation may pinpoint the leak to a specific component.
A continuous emissions monitoring system (CEMS) Observation may quantify the emission rate over time.

clause.p: All three Observations can serve as evidence for a single Emission Event, each contributing to the determination of quantity, start time, or end time. The Observation’s ultimateFeatureOfInterest references a Feature — which may be a Source or any other Feature at a different spatial scale. The satellite Observation’s feature of interest may be a Feature representing an entire facility, while the OGI Observation’s feature of interest is a Source representing a single valve.

clause.title: EmissionML and OMS 3.0 Procedures
clause.p: OMS 3.0 defines a Procedure as “a description of steps performed” and introduces ObservingProcedure as a specialization that describes steps performed to determine the value of an observable property. EmissionML follows this same specialization pattern.

The EmissionML conceptual model introduces a Procedure class (stereotyped ISO 19156/2023) as the base class for process descriptions. DeterminationProcedure inherits from Procedure and describes the steps performed to determine a value — such as an emission quantity or temporal bound. This structural inheritance mirrors how ObservingProcedure specializes Procedure in OMS 3.0.

By making Procedure an explicit base class rather than a notional reference, EmissionML ensures that its process descriptions are structurally aligned with OMS 3.0 — not merely by naming convention, but by class hierarchy. This parallels the Feature/Source pattern: just as Source inherits from Feature to make ISO 19109 alignment concrete, DeterminationProcedure inherits from Procedure to make OMS 3.0 alignment concrete.

clause.title: Why Explicit Domain Modeling
clause.p: The alternative. One could model everything with generic Features and Observations — Sources as plain Features, emission data as Observation results, and relationships as ad-hoc properties. This approach uses only existing OGC standards without defining new classes.
The problem. Generic modeling loses domain semantics. Without EmissionML’s explicit classes, systems cannot:

li.p: distinguish a Source from any other Feature,
enforce that an emission has a Mechanism and an Intent,
trace the evidence chain from Observation to Emission Quantity to Emission Event,
validate that required relationships are present (e.g., every Emission Event must have a Source, a Mechanism, and a Determination Procedure).

dd.p: Systems exchanging EmissionML data share a common vocabulary. EmissionEvent, Source, Mechanism, and EmissionQuantity are unambiguous — they mean the same thing in every implementation.

Requirements can be formally specified and enforced. For example, Requirement 1 mandates that every Emission Event has a Source, a Mechanism, start time, quantity, and their corresponding Determination Procedures.

Domain experts work with emission concepts directly — emission events, sources, mechanisms — rather than mapping to and from generic abstractions. This reduces the cognitive burden and the risk of misinterpretation.

clause.p: Analogy. Just as CityGML defines explicit Building, Road, and Vegetation classes rather than using generic Features, EmissionML defines explicit emission domain classes. The General Feature Model provides the abstract foundation; EmissionML provides the domain-specific vocabulary that makes emission data interoperable and actionable.

note.p: The TC has approved Springer LNCS as the official document citation type.
Springer LNCS is widely used in technical and computer science journals and other publications
[n] Journal: Author Surname, A.: Title. Publication Title. Volume number, Issue number, Pages Used (Year Published)

references.p: [1] OGC: OGC Testbed 12 Annex B: Architecture. (2015).
